Hi, I'm Kevin Halbert and I'm back and as promised we're going to go through an ad breakdown. This is an ad I wrote that was created after I created the slideshow and the presentation of the video that you saw before. That presentation was a basic foundational education on the most valuable principles to use in copywriting. The must-haves that you must have if you want to be competitive and be the most highly effective at copywriting. Now as originally stated, the copywriting course was going to be written for and teach people how to write copy for sales copy mostly for emails. The more I thought about it the more that there would be too many components missing. It would give you some strategies and some techniques and stuff like that that would enable you to write only one type of email. I felt a lot better and I felt I could serve you better if I gave you a much larger, broader foundation of understanding of principles that are used in direct response copy that is very, very effective and you see it in every great copywriter's copy. And so I decided to give you that foundational, that larger foundational components of all the things that you can use to add to your toolbox to write effective converting copy. This piece of copy that I've written here doesn't use every piece. Like I said, I'm giving you more. I want you to have more. This piece uses a lot. I'm guessing somewhere in the neighborhood of 80% of the techniques that I taught you. And that was the reason for creating this was you could see it in action, see it weaved into a piece of copy and just get to a better understanding of it. Now this is not a piece of copy that you should be expecting to write yourself. And by the way, this is no what by no means a complete piece of copy. You're actually seeing a semi-polished draft. It's partway there. There's a lot of pieces and a lot of flaws in it. But you get to see how it puts together and there's no missing out on how these things interact and how they work together. And as I said before, when I created the foundational stuff, I had no idea what piece of copy I was going to write. I just knew what I felt that you needed to know and have in your knowledge base for whatever type of copy you might write. Now I did give you one type of theme, but you can use many different themes. And that theme that I was using was the theme of storytelling, the theme that your story is the story of the reader. And that entailed a lot of research and understanding who the reader is, who they are, where they come from, what are their hopes, their dreams, their fears, their desires. Where's the dream destination they want to be, at, what's the words they use to describe where they're coming from, and etc. Now when I got to the end of the last foundational video email, I told you that I wanted to, that I went through the yellow pages. And what I basically did was I flipped through the yellow pages thinking about teaching you the theme of storytelling. I went through and chose businesses. Now I chose, you know, the thing is you can believe that I didn't write this copy before I gave you the foundational course because it's for a fictitious client. I can choose anybody I want. So it's not that hard to find something to make something fit. And I chose the most powerful service or product or service that I could find in the yellow pages as a way to illustrate for you what I'm about to teach you. And but of all the pieces I showed you, I showed you in the last video all the different businesses I considered using. And when you looked at that, what was common among all the pieces of the types of businesses, the categories they were in and the specific product or service they offered was that there was a high contrast. There was a high contrast of a story, meaning that people that hired people or paid for service, products and services that got those types of products and services, the results varied greatly. They could they could go from a very tragic experience to a very triumphant experience and there could be a very dramatic story involved. There was a high contrast. So you know, one of the one of the businesses I didn't leave in there, I don't know why it didn't stay in my slides, but one of the businesses that I would have actually chose other than some of the other ones, which might have seemed more powerful, was to be a wedding planner where I would have probably impersonate a ghost right for a female. But in any case, the point being is that a wedding planner, the result of wedding planning, the results are traumatic. They can be tragic to triumphant and there can be a big emotional story weaved in there. But in the end, I think plastic surgery is even more so because I mean, you might have the memory of a big day and it might bother you for a while and you might get over it. So if you got bad plastic surgery, that's something you're wearing on your face. That's the result you have to live with every day unless you do something about it and get it fixed. And so there's a much more drama, much bigger story to be told there. So that's what I decided to go with when it came into this piece of copy. Now I said in the previous video that I was talking about doing the research and going into the forums and finding out what their fears and desires and you know, all that stuff is and I'm not kidding. When I went into those forums and when I typed in, I believe I typed in facelift forum and I typed in for a facelift forum and the only forums I went in were the top three ranked organic facelift forums. And when I went into those forums, it was a treasure trove. I mean, it was amazing. And when I was reading through these people's stories and reading into their dialogue between each other, this is like I said, it's between the patients, not between the patients and the doctors, but it's the people that are having a frank discussion between themselves of what their outcome was, what they felt about the surgeries they had, what went down that was bad, what went down that was good, what their recommendations and cautions were and everything else. And the more I read that stuff, I got sucked in like nobody's business for a while there. I felt like I was planning my own plastic surgery. It was just that deep and intense and emotional and it was incredible. And it would really put me in the shoes of a person that's considering getting plastic surgery. And by the way, this story that we're about to tell, you know, we talk about what we want them to do. When we get into a story like this, we are asking ourselves, what do we want the reader to do when they're done reading this piece of copy? And in this case, what we want them to do is a couple of things. We want them to start looking at us as the person they want to go to for if they're considering getting plastic surgery. We also want them to sign up on our email list and we also want to send them more valuable content that will bond them and to us even further. So that's the objective of this copy that we're writing here right now. So with that, as the end goal, do you remember the Hemingway Challenge that we started off with? If you remember the Hemingway Challenge, the words that I chose on the Hemingway Challenge that should give you an idea of where this theme is going is, the Hemingway Challenge, the six word story is, plastic surgeon becomes patient, clients raving. That's our Hemingway Challenge. And that's the theme that we have going on here. Now if you think about that, what am I doing? I'm taking something where people are feeling when I was in the forums, they feel the divide between being a patient and being that patient who is at the mercy of the physician that they're dealing with. And the way that I decided to deal with this, the best way to describe it is that the patient is not feeling heard, not feeling understood, is feeling alone, is feeling fearful, is hopeful that things will turn out, they have concerns over pain, and a whole bunch of, a whole host of other things that we'll get into with this copy. But the question you have to ask yourself, if you were sitting here like me and you were faced with teaching people how to write copy and write a course, well, I have no rules upon me. I can write anything I want. And that's what my dad and John Carlton and people would do together, is they would come up with the most outrageous story they could tell, and they'd tell that story. And they would just let their minds free flow. And then what they would do is they would reign it back into where it was accurate and it was legal and it was truthful and everything else. But they didn't let their imagination stop them. And so if I'm going to write a piece of copy, I'm writing a piece of copy that is, if I were going to hire a plastic surgeon, what would I want his story to be? What would his ultimate story be that no one else could tell me a different plastic surgeon story that would trump what I'm telling? And that's what we've got here. And by the way, I wrote this piece of copy in three single sittings and I think I pretty much wrote it in a single, in a straight line format, which is kind of uncommon. It was common for my dad. I mean, I remember as a kid getting in a car, driving down to a client from LA to, you know, some place, an hour or two south of where we were, and he would get in with a blank legal pad and I'd be the one driving. He'd be the one riding in the car and then we'd pull up to the driveway of who the client was. We'd get out of the car and he'd have the copy done for the client. Now that was an incredible feat if you ask any other copywriter. And that's not normally how I write. I kind of got lucky on this one where I just sat down. I kind of, and I think, remember we talked about how you do the research and the copy kind of writes itself. That's what this felt like. It felt like after I did all the research, the copy was really writing itself. And so I did that. And the other thing is, is that if you see this copy, I didn't write it from some, so far out there, there was an astronomical thing. You know, this guy wasn't the father of plastic surgery. He coined the term and started it when there was no such thing in the world or anything like that. But I did write it from, excuse me, the most powerful form, position I think I could come from in a truthful and believable manner. And when you find out what the story is, if you think about it while we're going through the copy, just don't let it distract you from the education I'm giving you. But if you think about it from where I'm talking about, going through with all of this, you will realize that this copy is not outrageous. It is not beyond reality. And that you could plug this into so many surgeon story, plastic surgeon story or cosmetic surgeon stories. This can fit so many doctors. And if you write this out, this can kick ass on any other cosmetic surgery practice. And one of the things you're going to find is this kind of copy is going to be very different and very powerful to the kinds of copy you see. Ads that are run for cosmetic surgeons in local newspapers or on their own landing page and stuff, most of those tend to look like well laid out spas. They look like you're going to go to a day spa and get your cosmetic surgery. They're pretty stock photo pictures of implied half naked people that are impossibly beautiful and they look like they're in some Zen spa world. And none of it has this level of brochure look, photo brochure look where this is more of a who's behind it, who's operating on my face, who's, you know, what is, you know, just more of a behind the scenes and getting to know the person and understanding them and understanding their story. And that's a huge preamble I'm just giving you on getting started and getting into this piece of copy. But I think it's important. And so with our Hemingway challenge, I think you guys can kind of get an idea of where I'm coming from and what kind of stance I'm going to take and you ask yourself, and that's fine if there's a better answer, but what is, you know, what story would be stronger than this that would get you to choose a different cosmetic surgeon other than obviously location and price? Well, yeah. So anyways, let's get started. Okay. So the headline starts, Plastic Surgeon Gets a Taste of his Own Menacing. Now this is a curiosity based headline. And the thing going on here is we're pulling in two types of people into this body copy by using this headline. This is our attention. This is our ADA, Attention Interest Desire and Action. Bon reminded me that I did not mention ADA, Attention Interest Desire and Action. But when I looked back, I went, well, I taught them all that. I just didn't give them an acronym to use, which was remember we talked about our headline reaching out of the page, grabbing them and shaking them like a rag doll out of their coma. You know, by the way, that was a metaphor to make things more dramatic. So it was a copy I was using to help you get to know this, learn this easier. And also, so we had the attention. We talked about what are their biggest, their selfish interests and their biggest desires and stuff like that. So we definitely had the A and the I, the attention, the I is the interest and desires with biggest desires. And then we had our actions, our call to actions, what we want them to do and stuff like that. And ADA thing is a great way to think. It's a checklist that you must make sure that you are doing these things. But you can't really alienate them if you really get this stuff in your bones, if you really figure out that you, you know, this is how you're going to write copy. If you're adopting the direct response copywriters, if you're reading from any of the good direct response copywriters, playbooks, ADA is in there naturally whether or not it's spelled out for you or not. And it will become part of your, let's call it lexicon. Okay. So like I said, what we're looking at here is a curiosity headline, but we're also, we're calling out, we're actually calling out the people that have ever considered getting plastic surgery. How could someone who's considering getting plastic surgery ignore this? Because people are going to want to know what kind of taste of his own medicine, what, what is he, what is he dishing out that he is now receiving? Okay. And this is also going to, but this is also going to pull people into reading this copy. And we might, and it doesn't hurt to do this as long as it's a byproduct, as long as it doesn't dilute the strength of the, the headline, we're going to pull in people that maybe we're never even thinking about it. Cause it's, it's an article type, newsy curiosity type headline. So we could pull people in and just have a side benefit of people that never really thought about it or weren't thinking about it seriously or anything like that. And as you'll see the copy kind of turns around and gets some people that maybe were against plastic surgery into getting it. But anyone that's even remotely considering getting plastic surgery would have to read this. It would be one more thing that they'd have to know and you know, make sure that there's not some risk or some danger that they're not considering that they need to get clear on before and get clear of whatever hidden fear of unknown that might be contained in this copy. Remember, we talked about the shark and the water. So they'd have to get to know that. So it's just an interest piece. It's a curiosity piece. It's a self interest piece because a person is going to want to know if there's something that they should know about before they go through with getting plastic surgery. Okay. It goes first, first sentences. Even though I had plenty of excuses like med school and then a busy practice, I had to admit the real reason I put off having plastic surgery was because I was scared. Okay. So here is a person that is being coming forward, being forthright. He's admitting his flaws and his weaknesses. And we have this, but we're, we're hitting, we're going into this, this storytelling, this media you type of positioning. And so we're, we're following with the teachings of that. Okay. Now here's an important part. It's okay. Who are you? What are you, you know, and so here's what, what is your expert at? What do you, you know, what, what unique qualifications do you have? It says, my name is Dr. Harris and I've been a cosmetic surgeon in the Chicago area for 23 years. Okay. Hey, you know, he's obsessed, obviously a successful surgeon. If he's been able to last for 23 years, at least some sort of, some form of success. I've always taken a lot of pride in my work and raising the, and raising the quality of people's lives. I, when I read these out loud, by the way, this is an important thing is that, like I said, this is an unpolished copy, reading copy aloud really fine. Uh, gets you those stumbling places. When you write copy and you read it three, four or five times, you end up skimming it and you don't really read it like a person that's never read the copy before, but reading it out loud helps you do that. So the two, two tricks that every great copywriter I know of walks away from the copy, comes back and reads it later, but they also read it out loud. And the other thing is you have other people read it and that's a big important thing. If you can think of the best copywriters on the planet, I promise you they have other people, people they trust, family and friends read their copy to find out, um, obvious omissions and blunders and stuff that they, um, are too close to the subject to see. Okay. So here's a person that's always taken a lot of pride in their work and, uh, uh, in my work and raising the quality of people's lives. I know that sounds funny coming from a plastic surgeon instead of a teacher or a missionary. Now this is, this is coming in for any, you know, this is the, we're addressing the naysayer, the naysayer that's going, well, you're not Mother Teresa, you're not a missionary, you know, you're raising quality of people's lives. Are you protecting them from nuclear disaster and poisonous food and water and, and criminal elements and stuff like that. Um, but this is kind of a, just a disarming, you know, in the scale of things, you know, that it sounds funny, but I knew I was making people feel better about their looks. Okay. This is a person that has confidence. People don't want to go to a plastic surgeon that maybe hopes they can do better. They want a plastic surgeon that knows they can do better. Think about it. If you were having a heart surgery, heart surgery, you would not want someone that's really hoping things will turn out well. You want that guy that arrogant doctor with a guy complex that knows he can get the job done, won't, doesn't consider failure and option and stuff like that. Now you normally want a doctor that has a good bedside manner, but you want one that will take no prisoners and will, you know, stops it, nothing to get the job done right. Okay. So anyways, but I knew I was making people feel better about their looks. Okay. These are all, um, just taking pride and, uh, no, no, no, in no way lacking confidence. Now these things between these lines are sometimes what I find in copy that I do this often as I'll put between these two lines, a piece of copy that sometimes in this, in, it depends on what I'm thinking about. And sometimes to keep it and sometimes I eliminate it. What it means is, well, does that interrupt the flow of the copy or does it keep it going and does it transition nicely? Or is it a distraction that I have to like pull myself back in? Remember we talked about the Hemingway challenge. By the way, when the Hemingway challenge, the, the six words is, is a great rule. The person else who might read the Hemingway challenge may not get the theme that I'm talking about, but what it does do is it makes it very lean and efficient. If I were to expand the Hemingway challenge in this case, it would have been, um, something to do with, uh, if I would extend it to a few more words, it would be top plastic surgeon, um, professor or teacher of other plastic surgeons becomes, becomes, uh, patient. And now his clients are raving, pounding and beating in his door to get it, get it fit into his business, busy appointment schedule. So the Hemingway challenge isn't something where you got to go, well, it's got to be perfect. It can only fit in six and stuff like that. But the point is to be lean and efficient. And as we said with the Hemingway challenge, your point is to expand it out, you expand that sentence out or expand with more sentences, but always keep it that it's a lean straight line straight to your, straight to your call to action at the end. And so that whenever you're widening that, widening that story, um, you're always making the story stronger and not going down some, um, taking some, taking some wild side road. Okay. So anyways, um, so we got reason why the reason I was scared was because I'd seen firsthand how bad things could really get. So here's a guy who's really proud of what he does, but it doesn't always turn out that way. It can turn out really bad. So here, you tell me, right? I talked about picking something that has a high contrast that can turn out good or bad. You want people in a lot of instances to understand that how, how big a contrast the outcome of their decision can go is that it can go really bad and can go really well. Okay. You see, I was the go to guy when it came to fixing other people's blunders. Okay. Here we got this guy. He's the doctor of the doctors. Not only is that not as a great doctor. He's the one that people turn to in their most desperate hour. Okay. I mistakenly thought it was enough just to fix the damage caused by others. And you'll see this in my copy and other people's copy. This is a setup for something. It's comedians do this. They'll say, you know, if you ever noticed when you're talking to your spouse, you know, and when you do this, they do that. And then they go into the story. And so what they're doing is the person actually knows what the punchline is basically going to be before he gets there. And it kind of pre arms them for the laughter for the release of the tension of the joke when the punchline comes, because they've already kind of kind of got an idea where the rest of the story is going to go. So I mistakenly thought it was enough just to fix the damage caused by others. Translation is it's not enough just to fix the damage caused by others, usually by surgeons who weren't board certified. So this is this is saying two things. It's saying there are a bunch of blundering surgeons out there who are quacks and don't do what they do. But it's also saying I'm a board certified surgeon in a in a unspoken subtext. Okay, it wasn't until we came a patient when under the knife myself that I realized just what my patients were going through. Now here's someone who's at least telling a story that makes him unique to all the other physicians. He's a physician that is starting to begin a story of seeing both sides. He's on the side of the person that feels vulnerable and is on the other side is hopeful and feels how much is writing on the results of what he's about to do to them for which they basically have no control. And so this is this is something that you know, how can someone choose a surgeon that doesn't know what they're going through? I mean, trust me, they in many cases they could choose someone who doesn't know who they're going through and they would choose an awful experience with a physician if their results were better. But if they can have it all, we're talking about writing the ultimate copy, that's what you'd want. You want someone that had both. Okay, but all that changed when I could no longer put off my own surgery. Okay, so we got this, we got the setup again and we got a second setup. The reason I could no longer put off my own surgery was because my busy workload was catching up to me and it started to show on my face. Okay, so what we're doing here is we're forcing a person into a corner where the reader can start to understand that he had no choice. It was pressing upon him. Okay, I felt like my pre-op consult started to get this look in their eyes, ziff to say, if you're such a big fan of plastic surgery and downplay the risks, why haven't you had work done yourself? Okay, so then came the day when my thoughts were no longer just a suspicion. One day I was doing another consult. Now, remember we talked about using words, so I've used pre-op and I've used consult. And the thing is, is you want him to sound like a doctor. He's two things. He's a patient also, but he's also a doctor. So you want him to talk like a doctor somewhat. But you know he's a doctor. But you get that feeling that he has this experience, this authority and stuff like that. And this is the lingo that they would use. Okay, answering the user questions about, now here's an important thing. This maybe sounds like it's trivial, but it's been slipped in in person. Remember we talked about going through the forums and finding out what their biggest concerns and all that stuff is? This is a guy that's gone through those answers time and time again. Scars, anesthesia, cost, healing time. And suddenly he cut me off and said, and the truth is I should have this in itals. Itals, itals and quotes are very powerful. If you have the same copy and you can italicize it as a quote or what's called a pull quote, it has a stronger believability. It has a realism sense to it. So this should be italicized. If having a facelift is so safe and almost always turns out looking so great, why haven't you had it done? And I'll never forget, he went on to say. And the thing is, is this is we're using this very literal, we're giving them a reason why the reader can believe this, that this is literally the words they used instead of, so no offense doc. And that's kind of the kind of talky language. That's you know, the way a person would talk. But you look like you could use a facelift more than me. So we have this sneaking feeling and now we're back against the wall where it's a reality. We had a feeling about it and now it's a cold reality. By the way, by the way he was talking, I could tell he had already decided not to become a patient, but he was still waiting for an answer. And that makes him feel that pressure. I was stunned. I could feel my face goes flush. I was speechless. I thought that people were judging my confidence in plastic surgery was no longer just a suspicion. For at least some, it was a fact. Okay, so this is just storytelling, right? We're getting to it. And this is emotional. As angry as I was, deep down I knew he was right. Right then and there I decided to go under the knife. It only took me a moment to decide who would be performing the procedure. So we've moved on from him being back down the wall to what is he going to do now? He's back against the wall. Back then I was a cocky SOB with the biggest God complex. Remember we talked about a guy that thinks he's great. We don't want a guy that knows he's great, not that thinks he's okay. Because I was the guy to whom everyone came when everything came to when everything went wrong. Now I've talked about this before and you can see this in infomercials and stuff like that. You can see that infomercials actually are on a loop. They kind of change up the editing slightly but basically you're watching the same clips three and four and five times on an infomercial. And they find it doesn't hurt to restate what you're trying to say to them and what you're trying to sell to them. You don't have to go, well you already said that, don't say it again. Many times people want to be told more than once to really just give them that, are you sure? Yeah, I'm sure. Kind of feeling. That can be when people ask people out on dates and people promising that the car they're buying is going to be good or that there's still a warranty with it or stuff like that. Sometimes hearing it more than once really can drive it home. And there's a great proof of that is people that get mailed the same promotions in direct response, get the same promotion letter in the mail and don't buy until they've got the third or fifth letter. So we know that repetition can sell. Because I was the guy to whom everyone came to when everything went wrong. And more importantly the surgeon, other practicing surgeons waited in line for years to apprentice under was me. So remember we talked about who would be the best of the best. Would you want the guy that's, you know, does plastic surgery or would you want to get it from the guy that taught him how to do it? So I knew when it came time for me to go under the knife it was going to be confirmed by someone trained by me. Now the element we're looking for here is we're trying to find somebody that's good enough to operate on him because he can't operate on himself. But how do we get that person not to be someone who's above who he is? And by the way I had my mother and my brother Bond read this copy and remember I talked about the value of people reading copy. When I went through this copy and Bond called me back, you know, it's not a kindness to just say it's good, it's kindness to give an honest opinion about a problem if there is one. I can say that Bond said when he was reading the copy he felt more like he wanted to hire the person that operated on the physician in the story. And I said, you know, in many cases if there's someone who has an issue with something in the copy there's many others that could have that problem. And I think the key word is could. And the thing is when I put it to my mother she did not see it that way at all. Now if this was real copy going out to the rest of the world I had more people read it and if I found a significant number were feeling the way Bond was then the copy would need to be changed. And we'll get into some of the funnier, I'll get into a little bit of the funnier thing that happened after that when all three of us were together discussing this copy. So and I knew who my brightest pupil was. I trained her several years ago and she's now had a thriving practice in Dallas. I might as well tell you this story now. So she had a thriving practice in Dallas. Remember he's in Chicago. Remember what I did was I mailed her off to the opposite side of the country. And when I talked to my brother and my mom I said, well I could kill her. You know, I could make her die or I could send her off into a third world country helping cure people of cleft palates and hair lips or I could give her a nervous disorder where she can't hold a knife more accurately. And that was kind of funny. We all laughed about it and stuff like that. At the same time with just the thought was that it didn't diminish. And I could have put it in this copy which is one of the pieces of advice is make sure you get a doctor who's in your area because when complications arrive you're not going to have the convenience of boarding a plane or staying in a hotel for extended period of time for a year or six months or three weeks as you recover. But anyways that was the concept with dealing with it. But I think the more the idea was an idea of abundance and that if this bleed had bled off and got some patients to this other doctor that would be fine. I don't think it would hurt the results of a physician operating in the Chicago area. So you said it would be an honor and would perform the procedure for free. This is an honor. This is a person that looks up to this man. She's asking that she be able to show her patients my before and after videos and let them know I was her teacher. All of this is still holding up this physician up on a pedestal. Okay. Being in the same business I completely understood why and besides wanting to be operated on I think it should be by the best person I ever trained to see reading it out loud. I quickly agreed. Starting both of our schedules we set a surgery date for three months later. We're setting the scene again. We got three months later. So we got some anticipation of something coming. At first I didn't think much about the upcoming procedure. I felt a sense of relief just committing myself to doing it. But as the day grew nearer, see we already knew at first I didn't think much. At first they didn't. That means they're going to think about it later. But as the day grew nearer I grew increasingly anxious. Now the concerns and fears of my consults became my own. I would say became more and more my own. It would probably be added this growing anxiety. To be honest at least in my mind my fears and concerns were even greater. Now here's a we'll get into it in this next paragraph. You see the standard fears and concerns people had was that their relationships and self-esteem were on the line. But as far as I was concerned theirs weren't on the line like mine. And the thing is your fears and concerns are more important than the other persons because it's you and you have your life to live with those fears and concerns and his is more important than yours. The way I saw it my career was on the line because I was getting the 60,000 mile overhaul full face lift and rhinoplasty nose job. And you notice I put the rhinoplasty in here just to make it a more doctory. I could have left that out and just said nose job. I was going to be the post-child either for or against cosmetic surgery. So we're putting this drama in here of making the reader feel the real anxiety. This guy is feeling. We're putting them in the scene. I was going to go into the pre-op consults looking like the envy of everyone my age even younger than people who are actually younger than I am. Now this is something. Remember we talked about the contrast. This is the high side of the contrast. How cool would it be to look younger than people that are younger than you? Or here's the contrast. I was going to end up looking like a ventriloquist dummy in a wind tunnel. If you think about it remember we talked about metaphors. This is something where they could almost chuckle and laugh to themselves at it. And that's funny because it's scary. It's funny because they've seen people that kind of look like that. Celebrities that had the worst surgeries and stuff. So we talked about metaphors and analogies. This is something where we talk about how it can dramatize something or make something simpler to understand. It's kind of got that piece that you say that it's easy to remember. It's like that if the glove won't fit. Would you ever hire someone who looked like that to operate on them? So you're asking a question. Remember questions are a very powerful thing. You need to use them in the right context. And the question when you leave a question mark, a question mark implies something. It implies unless it's rhetorical the question mark implies I want an answer. And if you want an answer what happens when someone asks you a question and you give an answer? You have to process it through your mind. You have to take it in, go into your imagination and your thought process for a moment and you have to come out with, you have to spit out a result. This is rhetorical but it's still got a question mark at the end and it still works as a search yourself for how you'd feel about it. ago that you're looking at right here that hasn't been written and so even though there's a bunch of surgeons out there and they're they're all offering their services and hanging their shingle out there and yelling you know from a megafall what they have to offer to the world none of them are doing it in this way and so even though you have thousands of other businesses that offer the same thing you offer you have the ability to offer a story even if it's the same as the guy next door story and bring in customers that they're not because they're not using this powerful storytelling technique and this copyright copywriting concepts and powers of persuasions to bring people into their door so no matter what your business is you can tell a story and it will bring you a bunch of customers that won't come in through anyone else's door any other way and will give you a uniqueness and a bond with people like no one else like none of your competition has to offer so I hope you sophisticated that blended all these concepts together and you could see them all working in concert together in action and see the value and the power that they have but then when you went into emails that's which were a much lighter diet version of this you would only use a handful of these components especially if you were in a providing bonding content you know stuff we warming up content where you're drip feeding them little pieces of gems of free advice and stuff like that where you're not fully on full force full frontal assault selling that you have a couple components in there but
